perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any of its associates, members, employees, or subcontractors, except those employees or subcontractors who are required to have the Confidential Information in order to perform their duties in connection with the evaluation and continuation of a business relationship between the Contractor and the Company.The Contractor shall inform each such individual of theproprietary nature of the Confidential Information and of the terms and obligations of this Agreem ent. Each permitted employee, subcontractor, member, or associate to whom Confidential Information is disclosed shall sign a non - disclosure agreement substantially the same as this Agreement at the request of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any of its associates, members, employees, or subcontractors, except those employees or subcontractors who are required to have the Confidential Information in order to perform their duties in connection with the evaluation and continuation of a business relationship between the Contractor and the Company. The Contractor is not required to inform each such individual of the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information or of the terms and obligations of this Agreement. Each permitted employee, subcontractor, member, or associate to whom Confidential Information is disclosed shall sign a non - disclosure agreement substantially the same as this Agreement at the request of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a direct contradiction regarding the Contractor's responsibility to inform its personnel about the confidential nature of the information. Section 1c originally requires the Contractor to inform each individual. The modified text explicitly states the Contractor is not required to do so. This undermines the purpose of ensuring confidentiality, as personnel may unknowingly mishandle confidential information, and creates uncertainty about enforcement. This contradicts the expectation that the associates will maintain confidentiality, particularly given the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: c) Applicability to Employees and Subcontractors . The Contractor shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any of its associates, members, employees, or subcontractors, except those employees or subcontractors who are required to have the Confidential Information in order to perform their duties in connection with the evaluation and continuation of a business relationship between the Contractor and the Company. The Contractor is not required to inform each such individual of the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information or of the terms and obligations of this Agreement. Each permitted employee, subcontractor, member, or associate to whom Confidential Information is disclosed shall sign a non - disclosure agreement substantially the same as this Agreement at the request of the Company.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text by stating that 'The Contractor is not required to inform each such individual of the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information or of the terms and obligations of this Agreement.' This directly contradicts the original requirement that the Contractor shall inform each individual of the proprietary nature of confidential information, creating an inconsistency as described in the Perturbation Explanation. The Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text, which shows the contradiction is in the document itself.

perturbation.original_text: Each permitted employee, subcontractor, member, or associate to whom Confidential Information is disclosed shall sign a non - disclosure agreement substantially the same as this Agreement at the request of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Each permitted employee, subcontractor, member, or associate to whom Confidential Information is disclosed may sign a non - disclosure agreement substantially the same as this Agreement at the request of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a contradiction by changing 'shall' to 'may' in relation to signing a non-disclosure agreement. The original text mandates that anyone receiving confidential information must sign the NDA. The changed text makes it optional, which directly contradicts the purpose of maintaining strict confidentiality outlined in section 1a. This undermines the legal protection the Company seeks and creates uncertainty about whether the NDA is actually enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a) Company Information . The Contractor agrees at all times during the term of its contract with th e Company (the “Term”) and thereafter, to hold in strictest confidence, and not to use, except for the benefit of the Company, or to disclose to any person, firm, or corporation without written authorization of the Company, any Confidential Information of the Company. “Confidential Information” means any Company proprietary information, technical data, trade secrets or know -how, including, but not limited to, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, formulas, technology, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, finances or other business information disclosed to the Contractor by the Company either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated that employees, subcontractors, members, or associates 'shall' sign a non-disclosure agreement, ensuring confidentiality. The changed text replaces 'shall' with 'may,' making the NDA optional. This directly contradicts section 'a)', which requires the Contractor to 'hold in strictest confidence' and not disclose Confidential Information. If signing an NDA is optional, it undermines the 'strictest confidence' requirement and creates an inconsistency regarding the obligation to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor recognizes that the Company has received and in the future will receive from third parties their confidential or proprietary information subject to a duty on the Company’s part to maintain the confidentiality of such information and to use it only for cer tain limited purposes, and hereby agrees to hold all such confidential or proprietary information in the strictest confidence and not to disclose it to any person, firm, or corporation or to use it except as necessary in carrying out its work for theCompan y consistent with the Company’s agreement with such third party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor recognizes that the Company has received and in the future will receive from third parties their confidential or proprietary information subject to a duty on the Company’s part to maintain the confidentiality of such information and to use it only for cer tain limited purposes, and hereby agrees to hold all such confidential or proprietary information with reasonable confidence and is allowed to disclose it to any person, firm, or corporation as necessary in carrying out its work for the Company, irrespective of the Company’s agreement with such third party.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the obligation to protect third-party confidential information. The original text mandates 'strictest confidence,' aligning the Contractor's duty with the Company's. The modification replaces this with 'reasonable confidence' and removes the restriction on disclosure based on the Company's agreement with the third party. This creates an in-text contradiction with the original intention of protecting third-party information. It contradicts the spirit of confidentiality established in Section 1a.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a) Company Information . The Contractor agrees at all times during the term of its contract with th e Company (the “Term”) and thereafter, to hold in strictest confidence, and not to use, except for the benefit of the Company, or to disclose to any person, firm, or corporation without written authorization of the Company, any Confidential Information of the Company. “Confidential Information” means any Company proprietary information, technical data, trade secrets or know -how, including, but not limited to, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, formulas, technology, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, finances or other business information disclosed to the Contractor by the Company either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the confidentiality obligations. The original text requires 'strictest confidence,' mirroring the Company's duty. The changed text replaces this with 'reasonable confidence' and allows disclosure irrespective of the Company's agreement. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (section a), which mandates 'strictest confidence' and prohibits disclosure without written authorization from the Company. The changed text makes the obligations incompatible, creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined in Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At the end of the Term, the Contractor will return to the Company, retaining nocopies or notes, all documents relating to the Company’s business including, but not limit ed to,reports, abstracts, lists, correspondence, information, computer files, computer disks, and allother materials and all copies of such material, obtained by the Contractor during itscontractual relationship with the company.
perturbation.changed_text: At the end of the Term, the Contractor will return to the Company, retaining copies of notes, all documents relating to the Company’s business including, but not limit ed to,reports, abstracts, lists, correspondence, information, computer files, computer disks, and allother materials and all copies of such material, obtained by the Contractor during itscontractual relationship with the company.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the requirement to return all materials without retaining copies. By changing 'retaining no copies or notes' to 'retaining copies of notes', it allows the Contractor to keep notes related to the Confidential Information, undermining the Company's intention to have full control over its proprietary information after the termination of the contract. This directly impacts Section 1a, which aims to prevent any unauthorized use of Confidential Information, even after the contract ends.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a) Company Information . The Contractor agrees at all times during the term of its contract with th e Company (the “Term”) and thereafter, to hold in strictest confidence, and not to use, except for the benefit of the Company, or to disclose to any person, firm, or corporation without written authorization of the Company, any Confidential Information of the Company. “Confidential Information” means any Company proprietary information, technical data, trade secrets or know -how, including, but not limited to, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, formulas, technology, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, finances or other business information disclosed to the Contractor by the Company either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original requirement that the Contractor return all documents and materials without retaining copies to allow them to retain copies of notes. This directly contradicts section 1a, which requires the Contractor to hold all Confidential Information in strictest confidence and not use or disclose it, even after the term of the contract. Allowing the contractor to retain copies of notes creates a conflict and undermines the confidentiality clause, fitting the definition of inconsistencies leading to in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor recognizes that the Company may be irreparably damaged by anybreach of this Agreement and that the Company shall be entitled to seek an injunction, specificperformance, or other equitable remedy to prevent such co mpetition or disclosure, and mayentitle the Company to other legal remedies, including attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor recognizes that the Company may be irreparably damaged by anybreach of this Agreement and that the Company shall be entitled to seek an injunction, specificperformance, or other equitable remedy to prevent such co mpetition or disclosure, and will not entitle the Company to other legal remedies, including attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: The change introduces a direct contradiction regarding the Company's entitlement to legal remedies. Originally, the Company 'may' be entitled to legal remedies, including attorney's fees. By changing this to 'will not entitle' the Company to such remedies, it removes a potential legal recourse for the Company in case of a breach. This directly contradicts the purpose of Section 1, which aims to protect the Company's Confidential Information and establishes legal remedies that might be pursued by the Company in case of breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a) Company Information . The Contractor agrees at all times during the term of its contract with th e Company (the “Term”) and thereafter, to hold in strictest confidence, and not to use, except for the benefit of the Company, or to disclose to any person, firm, or corporation without written authorization of the Company, any Confidential Information of the Company. “Confidential Information” means any Company proprietary information, technical data, trade secrets or know -how, including, but not limited to, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, formulas, technology, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, finances or other business information disclosed to the Contractor by the Company either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text by stating the Company 'will not entitle' to other legal remedies, including attorney's fees and costs. This contradicts the contradicted text, which implies the Company's right to protect Confidential Information and seek remedies in case of a breach. The change removes a legal recourse that the Company might pursue, creating an inconsistency with the aim of protecting confidential information as per the contradicted text. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists, adhering to the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.
